title: World Wide Web

infobox.value: (also known as **WWW**, **W3**, or simply the **Web**)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a public interconnected [information system](/wiki/Information_system "Information system") that enables [content](/wiki/Content_(media) "Content (media)") sharing over the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet").[[3]](#cite_note-3) It facilitates access to documents and other [web resources](/wiki/Web_resource "Web resource") according to specific rules of the [Hypertext Transfer Protocol](/wiki/HTTP "HTTP") (HTTP).[[4]](#cite_note-webinternet-4)

Linked hypertext system on the Internet

"Internet surfing" redirects here. For the underlying network, see [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"). "WWW" and "The Web" redirect here. For other uses, see [WWW (disambiguation)](/wiki/WWW_(disambiguation) "WWW (disambiguation)") and [The Web (disambiguation)](/wiki/The_Web_(disambiguation) "The Web (disambiguation)"). A [web page](/wiki/Web_page "Web page") from [Wikipedia](/wiki/Wikipedia "Wikipedia") displayed in [Google Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome "Google Chrome")

The **World Wide Web** (also known as **WWW**, **W3**, or simply the **Web**)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a public interconnected [information system](/wiki/Information_system "Information system") that enables [content](/wiki/Content_(media) "Content (media)") sharing over the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet").[[3]](#cite_note-3) It facilitates access to documents and other [web resources](/wiki/Web_resource "Web resource") according to specific rules of the [Hypertext Transfer Protocol](/wiki/HTTP "HTTP") (HTTP).[[4]](#cite_note-webinternet-4)

The Web was invented by English computer scientist [Tim Berners-Lee](/wiki/Tim_Berners-Lee "Tim Berners-Lee") while at [CERN](/wiki/CERN "CERN") in 1989 and opened to the public in 1993. It was conceived as a "universal linked information system".[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-TBL_Web_Proposal-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Documents and other media content are made available to the network through [web servers](/wiki/Web_server "Web server") and can be accessed by programs such as [web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser"). Servers and resources on the World Wide Web are identified and located through a character string called [uniform resource locator](/wiki/Uniform_resource_locator "Uniform resource locator") (URL).

The original and still very common document type is a [web page](/wiki/Web_page "Web page") formatted in [Hypertext Markup Language](/wiki/Hypertext_Markup_Language "Hypertext Markup Language") (HTML). This markup language supports [plain text](/wiki/Plain_text "Plain text"), [images](/wiki/Digital_image "Digital image"), embedded [video](/wiki/Video "Video") and [audio](/wiki/Audio_signal "Audio signal") contents, and [scripts](/wiki/Scripting_language "Scripting language") (short programs) that implement complex user interaction. The HTML language also supports [hyperlinks](/wiki/Hyperlink "Hyperlink") (embedded URLs), which provide immediate access to other web resources. [Web navigation](/wiki/Web_navigation "Web navigation"), or web surfing, is the common practice of following such hyperlinks across multiple websites. [Web applications](/wiki/Web_application "Web application") are web pages that function as [application software](/wiki/Application_software "Application software"). The information on the Web is transferred across the Internet using HTTP. Multiple web resources with a common theme and usually a common [domain name](/wiki/Domain_name "Domain name") make up a [website](/wiki/Website "Website"). A single web server may provide multiple websites, while some websites, especially the most popular ones, may be provided by multiple servers. Website content is provided by a myriad of companies, organisations, government agencies, and [individual users](/wiki/User-generated_content "User-generated content"); and comprises an enormous amount of educational, entertainment, commercial, and government information.

The World Wide Web has become the world's dominant [information systems platform](/wiki/Web_platform "Web platform").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-internetlivestats-11) It is the primary tool that billions of people worldwide use to interact with the Internet.[[4]](#cite_note-webinternet-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the World Wide Web](/wiki/History_of_the_World_Wide_Web "History of the World Wide Web")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/First_Web_Server.jpg/250px-First_Web_Server.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_Web_Server.jpg)

This [NeXT Computer](/wiki/NeXT_Computer "NeXT Computer") was used by [Sir Tim Berners-Lee](/wiki/Sir_Tim_Berners-Lee "Sir Tim Berners-Lee") at [CERN](/wiki/CERN "CERN") and became the world's first [Web server](/wiki/Web_server "Web server").

The Web was invented by English computer scientist [Tim Berners-Lee](/wiki/Tim_Berners-Lee "Tim Berners-Lee") while working at [CERN](/wiki/CERN "CERN").[[12]](#cite_note-AHT-12)[[13]](#cite_note-samm2016-13) He was motivated by the problem of storing, updating, and finding documents and data files in that large and constantly changing organisation, as well as distributing them to collaborators outside CERN. In his design, Berners-Lee dismissed the common [tree structure](/wiki/Tree_(computing) "Tree (computing)") approach, used for instance in the existing CERNDOC documentation system and in the [Unix filesystem](/wiki/Unix_filesystem "Unix filesystem"), as well as approaches that relied on tagging files with [keywords](/wiki/Index_term "Index term"), as in the VAX/NOTES system. Instead, he adopted concepts he had put into practice with his private [ENQUIRE](/wiki/ENQUIRE "ENQUIRE") system (1980), built at CERN. When he became aware of [Ted Nelson](/wiki/Ted_Nelson "Ted Nelson")'s [hypertext](/wiki/Hypertext "Hypertext") model (1965), in which documents can be linked in unconstrained ways through [hyperlinks](/wiki/Hyperlink "Hyperlink") associated with "hot spots" embedded in the text, it helped to confirm the validity of his concept.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/WWW-LetShare.svg/250px-WWW-LetShare.svg.png)](/wiki/File:WWW-LetShare.svg)

The former World Wide Web logo, designed by [Robert Cailliau](/wiki/Robert_Cailliau "Robert Cailliau"). Currently, there is no widely accepted logo in use for the WWW.

The model was later popularised by [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Computer "Apple Computer")'s [HyperCard](/wiki/HyperCard "HyperCard") system. Unlike Hypercard, Berners-Lee's new system from the outset was meant to support links between multiple databases on independent computers, and to allow simultaneous access by many users from any computer on the Internet. He also specified that the system should eventually handle other media besides text, such as graphics, speech, and video. Links could refer to mutable data files, or even fire up programs on their server computer. He also conceived "gateways" that would allow access through the new system to documents organised in other ways (such as traditional computer [file systems](/wiki/File_system "File system") or the [Usenet](/wiki/Usenet "Usenet")). Moreover, he insisted that the system should be decentralised, without any central control or coordination over the creation of links.[[6]](#cite_note-TBL_Web_Proposal-6)[[16]](#cite_note-bern1992-16)[[12]](#cite_note-AHT-12)[[13]](#cite_note-samm2016-13)

Berners-Lee submitted a proposal to CERN in May 1989, without giving the system a name.[[6]](#cite_note-TBL_Web_Proposal-6) He got a working system implemented by the end of 1990, including a browser called [WorldWideWeb](/wiki/WorldWideWeb "WorldWideWeb") (which became the name of the project and of the network) and [an HTTP server](/wiki/CERN_httpd "CERN httpd") running at CERN. As part of that development, he defined the first version of the HTTP protocol, the basic URL syntax, and implicitly made HTML the primary document format.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The technology was released outside CERN to other research institutions starting in January 1991, and then to the whole Internet on 23 August 1991. The Web was a success at CERN and began to spread to other scientific and academic institutions. Within the next two years, [there were 50 websites created](/wiki/List_of_websites_founded_before_1995 "List of websites founded before 1995").[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

CERN made the Web protocol and code available royalty-free on 30 April 1993, enabling its widespread use.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) After the [NCSA](/wiki/National_Center_for_Supercomputing_Applications "National Center for Supercomputing Applications") released the [Mosaic web browser](/wiki/Mosaic_(web_browser) "Mosaic (web browser)") later that year, the Web's popularity grew rapidly as [thousands of websites](/wiki/List_of_websites_founded_before_1995 "List of websites founded before 1995") sprang up in less than a year.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Couldry-24) Mosaic was a graphical browser that could display inline images and submit [forms](/wiki/Form_(HTML) "Form (HTML)") that were processed by the [HTTPd server](/wiki/NCSA_HTTPd "NCSA HTTPd").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Marc Andreessen](/wiki/Marc_Andreessen "Marc Andreessen") and [Jim Clark](/wiki/James_H._Clark "James H. Clark") founded [Netscape](/wiki/Netscape "Netscape") the following year and released the [Navigator browser](/wiki/Netscape_Navigator "Netscape Navigator"), which introduced [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)") and [JavaScript](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript") to the Web. It quickly became the dominant browser. Netscape [became a public company](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") in 1995, which triggered a frenzy for the Web and started the [dot-com bubble](/wiki/Dot-com_bubble "Dot-com bubble").[[27]](#cite_note-27) Microsoft responded by developing its own browser, [Internet Explorer](/wiki/Internet_Explorer "Internet Explorer"), starting the [browser wars](/wiki/Browser_wars "Browser wars"). By bundling it with Windows, it became the dominant browser for 14 years.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Berners-Lee founded the [World Wide Web Consortium](/wiki/World_Wide_Web_Consortium "World Wide Web Consortium") (W3C) which created [XML](/wiki/XML "XML") in 1996 and recommended replacing HTML with stricter [XHTML](/wiki/XHTML "XHTML").[[29]](#cite_note-29) In the meantime, developers began exploiting an IE feature called [XMLHttpRequest](/wiki/XMLHttpRequest "XMLHttpRequest") to make [Ajax](/wiki/Ajax_(programming) "Ajax (programming)") applications and launched the [Web 2.0](/wiki/Web_2.0 "Web 2.0") revolution. [Mozilla](/wiki/Mozilla "Mozilla"), [Opera](/wiki/Opera_(company) "Opera (company)"), and Apple rejected XHTML and created the [WHATWG](/wiki/WHATWG "WHATWG") which developed [HTML5](/wiki/HTML5 "HTML5").[[30]](#cite_note-30) In 2009, the W3C conceded and abandoned XHTML.[[31]](#cite_note-31) In 2019, it ceded control of the HTML specification to the WHATWG.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The World Wide Web has been central to the development of the [Information Age](/wiki/Information_Age "Information Age") and is the primary tool billions of people use to interact on the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet").[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[11]](#cite_note-internetlivestats-11)

### Competition with Gopher

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Competition with Gopher")]

[Gopher](/wiki/Gopher_(protocol) "Gopher (protocol)") was run by the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota") and the alternative to the World Wide Web.

* In February 1993, the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota") announced that it would charge licensing fees for the use of its implementation of the Gopher server.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-gopher_protocol_rise_and_fall-37) Users became concerned that fees might also be charged for independent implementations.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) Gopher expansion stagnated, to the advantage of the World Wide Web which released the [WWW](/wiki/WWW "WWW") into the [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain") in [April 1993](/wiki/April_1993 "April 1993"),[[40]](#cite_note-40) to which [CERN](/wiki/CERN "CERN") [maintained](/wiki/Software_maintainer "Software maintainer").[[41]](#cite_note-41) In September 2000, the University of Minnesota re-licensed its Gopher software under the [GNU General Public License](/wiki/GNU_General_Public_License "GNU General Public License").[[42]](#cite_note-42)
* Gopher client functionality was quickly duplicated by the early [Mosaic](/wiki/Mosaic_(web_browser) "Mosaic (web browser)") web browser, which subsumed its protocol.
* Gopher has a more rigid structure than the free-form HyperText Markup Language ([HTML](/wiki/HTML "HTML")) of the Web. Every Gopher document has a defined format and type, and the typical user navigates through a single server-defined menu system to get to a particular document. This can be quite different from the way a user finds documents on the Web.
* Failure to follow the [open systems model](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") and bad publicity in comparison to the World Wide Web.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Nomenclature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Nomenclature")]

Tim Berners-Lee states that *World Wide Web* is officially spelled as three separate words, each capitalised, with no intervening hyphens.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Use of the www prefix has been declining, especially when [web applications](/wiki/Web_application "Web application") sought to brand their domain names and make them easily pronounceable. As the [mobile web](/wiki/Mobile_web "Mobile web") grew in popularity,[[45]](#cite_note-45) services like [Gmail.com](/wiki/Gmail "Gmail"), [Outlook.com](/wiki/Outlook.com "Outlook.com"), [Myspace](/wiki/Myspace "Myspace").com, [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook").com and [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter").com are most often mentioned without adding "www." (or, indeed, ".com") to the domain.[[46]](#cite_note-cas-46)

In English, *www* is usually read as *double-u double-u double-u*.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Some users pronounce it *dub-dub-dub*, particularly in New Zealand.[[48]](#cite_note-48) [Stephen Fry](/wiki/Stephen_Fry "Stephen Fry"), in his "Podgrams" series of podcasts, pronounces it *wuh wuh wuh*.[[49]](#cite_note-49) The English writer [Douglas Adams](/wiki/Douglas_Adams "Douglas Adams") once quipped in *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent") on Sunday* (1999): "The World Wide Web is the only thing I know of whose shortened form takes three times longer to say than what it's short for".[[50]](#cite_note-Sim-50)

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Function")]

Main articles: [HTTP](/wiki/HTTP "HTTP") and [HTML](/wiki/HTML "HTML")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Internet_Key_Layers.png/250px-Internet_Key_Layers.png)](/wiki/File:Internet_Key_Layers.png)

The World Wide Web functions as an [application layer](/wiki/Application_layer "Application layer") [protocol](/wiki/Communications_protocol "Communications protocol") that is run "on top of" (figuratively) the Internet, helping it to make it more functional. The advent of the [Mosaic](/wiki/Mosaic_(web_browser) "Mosaic (web browser)") web browser helped to make the web much more usable, including the display of images and moving images ([GIFs](/wiki/GIF "GIF")).

The terms *Internet* and *World Wide Web* are often used without much distinction. However, the two terms do not mean the same thing. The Internet is a global system of [computer networks](/wiki/Computer_network "Computer network") interconnected through telecommunications and [optical networking](/wiki/Optical_networking "Optical networking"). In contrast, the World Wide Web is a global collection of documents and other [resources](/wiki/Web_resource "Web resource"), linked by hyperlinks and [URIs](/wiki/Uniform_Resource_Identifier "Uniform Resource Identifier"). Web resources are accessed using [HTTP](/wiki/HTTP "HTTP") or [HTTPS](/wiki/HTTPS "HTTPS"), which are application-level Internet protocols that use the Internet transport protocols.[[4]](#cite_note-webinternet-4)

Viewing a [web page](/wiki/Web_page "Web page") on the World Wide Web normally begins either by typing the [URL](/wiki/Uniform_resource_locator "Uniform resource locator") of the page into a web browser or by following a hyperlink to that page or resource. The web browser then initiates a series of background communication messages to fetch and display the requested page. In the 1990s, using a browser to view web pages—and to move from one web page to another through hyperlinks—came to be known as 'browsing,' 'web surfing' (after [channel surfing](/wiki/Channel_surfing "Channel surfing")), or 'navigating the Web'. Early studies of this new behaviour investigated user patterns in using web browsers. One study, for example, found five user patterns: exploratory surfing, window surfing, evolved surfing, bounded navigation, and targeted navigation.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The following example demonstrates the functioning of a web browser when accessing a page at the URL `http://example.org/home.html`. The browser resolves the server name of the URL (`example.org`) into an [Internet Protocol address](/wiki/IP_address "IP address") using the globally distributed [Domain Name System](/wiki/Domain_Name_System "Domain Name System") (DNS). This lookup returns an IP address such as *203.0.113.4* or *2001:db8:2e::7334*. The browser then requests the resource by sending an [HTTP](/wiki/Hypertext_Transfer_Protocol "Hypertext Transfer Protocol") request across the Internet to the computer at that address. It requests service from a specific TCP port number that is well known for the HTTP service, so that the receiving host can distinguish an HTTP request from other network protocols it may be servicing. HTTP normally uses [port number 80](/wiki/List_of_TCP_and_UDP_port_numbers "List of TCP and UDP port numbers") and, for HTTPS, it normally uses [port number 443](/wiki/List_of_TCP_and_UDP_port_numbers "List of TCP and UDP port numbers"). The content of the HTTP request can be as simple as two lines of text:

```
GET /home.html HTTP/1.1
Host: example.org
```

The computer receiving the HTTP request delivers it to the web server software listening for requests on port 80. If the web server can fulfil the request, it sends an HTTP response back to the browser indicating success:

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
```

Followed by the content of the requested page. Hypertext Markup Language ([HTML](/wiki/HTML "HTML")) for a basic web page might look like this:

```
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Example.org – The World Wide Web</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <p>The World Wide Web, abbreviated as WWW and commonly known ...</p>
  </body>
</html>
```

The web browser [parses](/wiki/Parsing "Parsing") the HTML and interprets the markup (`<title>`, `<p>` for paragraph, and such) that surrounds the words to format the text on the screen. Many web pages use HTML to reference the URLs of other resources such as images, other embedded media, [scripts](/wiki/Client-side_scripting "Client-side scripting") that affect page behaviour, and [Cascading Style Sheets](/wiki/Cascading_Style_Sheets "Cascading Style Sheets") that affect page layout. The browser makes additional HTTP requests to the web server for these other [Internet media types](/wiki/Internet_media_type "Internet media type"). As it receives its content from the web server, the browser progressively [renders](/wiki/Browser_engine "Browser engine") the page onto the screen as specified by its HTML and these additional resources.

### HTML

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: HTML")]

Main article: [HTML](/wiki/HTML "HTML")

Hypertext Markup Language (HTML) is the standard [markup language](/wiki/Markup_language "Markup language") for creating [web pages](/wiki/Web_page "Web page") and [web applications](/wiki/Web_application "Web application"). With [Cascading Style Sheets](/wiki/Cascading_Style_Sheets "Cascading Style Sheets") (CSS) and [JavaScript](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript"), it forms a triad of [cornerstone](/wiki/Cornerstone "Cornerstone") technologies for the World Wide Web.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

[Web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") receive HTML documents from a [web server](/wiki/Web_server "Web server") or from local storage and [render](/wiki/Browser_engine "Browser engine") the documents into multimedia web pages. HTML describes the structure of a web page [semantically](/wiki/Semantic_Web "Semantic Web") and originally included cues for the appearance of the document.

[HTML elements](/wiki/HTML_element "HTML element") are the building blocks of HTML pages. With HTML constructs, [images](/wiki/HTML_element#Images_and_objects "HTML element") and other objects such as [interactive forms](/wiki/Fieldset "Fieldset") may be embedded into the rendered page. HTML provides a means to create [structured documents](/wiki/Structured_document "Structured document") by denoting structural [semantics](/wiki/Semantics "Semantics") for text such as headings, paragraphs, lists, [links](/wiki/Hyperlink "Hyperlink"), quotes, and other items. HTML elements are delineated by *tags*, written using [angle brackets](/wiki/Bracket#Angle_brackets "Bracket"). Tags such as `<img />` and `<input />` directly introduce content into the page. Other tags, such as `<p>`, surround and provide information about document text and may include other tags as sub-elements. Browsers do not display the HTML tags, but use them to interpret the content of the page.

HTML can embed programs written in a [scripting language](/wiki/Scripting_language "Scripting language") such as [JavaScript](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript"), which affects the behaviour and content of web pages. Inclusion of CSS defines the look and layout of content. The [World Wide Web Consortium](/wiki/World_Wide_Web_Consortium "World Wide Web Consortium") (W3C), maintainer of both the HTML and the CSS standards, has encouraged the use of CSS over explicit presentational HTML since 1997.[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit)[[53]](#cite_note-deprecated-53)

### Linking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Linking")]

Most web pages contain hyperlinks to other related pages and perhaps to downloadable files, source documents, definitions, and other web resources. In the underlying HTML, a hyperlink is coded like this:
`<a href="http://example.org/home.html">Example.org Homepage</a>.`

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/WorldWideWebAroundWikipedia.png/250px-WorldWideWebAroundWikipedia.png)](/wiki/File:WorldWideWebAroundWikipedia.png)

Graphic representation of a minute fraction of the WWW, demonstrating [hyperlinks](/wiki/Hyperlink "Hyperlink")

Such a collection of useful, related resources interconnected via hypertext links is dubbed a *web* of information. Publication on the Internet created what Tim Berners-Lee first called the *WorldWideWeb* (in its original [CamelCase](/wiki/CamelCase "CamelCase"), which was subsequently discarded) in November 1990.[[54]](#cite_note-W90-54)

The hyperlink structure of the web is described by the [webgraph](/wiki/Webgraph "Webgraph"): the nodes of the web graph correspond to the web pages (or URLs), the directed edges between them to the hyperlinks. Over time, many web resources pointed to by hyperlinks disappear, relocate, or are replaced with different content. This makes hyperlinks obsolete, a phenomenon referred to in some circles as link rot, and the hyperlinks affected by it are often called ["dead" links](/wiki/Link_rot "Link rot"). The ephemeral nature of the Web has prompted many efforts to archive websites. The [Internet Archive](/wiki/Internet_Archive "Internet Archive"), active since 1996, is the best known of such efforts.

### www prefix

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: www prefix")]

Many hostnames used for the World Wide Web begin with *www* because of the long-standing practice of naming [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") hosts according to the services they provide. The [hostname](/wiki/Hostname "Hostname") of a [web server](/wiki/Web_server "Web server") is often *www*, in the same way that it may be *ftp* for an [FTP server](/wiki/FTP_server "FTP server"), and *news* or *nntp* for a [Usenet](/wiki/Usenet "Usenet") [news server](/wiki/News_server "News server"). These hostnames appear as Domain Name System (DNS) or [subdomain](/wiki/Subdomain "Subdomain") names, as in *www.example.com*. The use of *www* is not required by any technical or policy standard and many websites do not use it; the first web server was *nxoc01.cern.ch*.[[55]](#cite_note-55) According to Paolo Palazzi, who worked at CERN along with Tim Berners-Lee, the popular use of *www* as subdomain was accidental; the World Wide Web project page was intended to be published at www.cern.ch while info.cern.ch was intended to be the CERN home page; however the DNS records were never switched, and the practice of prepending *www* to an institution's website domain name was subsequently copied.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Many established websites still use the prefix, or they employ other subdomain names such as *www2*, *secure* or *en* for special purposes. Many such web servers are set up so that both the main domain name (e.g., example.com) and the *www* subdomain (e.g., www.example.com) refer to the same site; others require one form or the other, or they may map to different websites. The use of a subdomain name is useful for [load balancing](/wiki/Load_balancing_(computing) "Load balancing (computing)") incoming web traffic by creating a [CNAME record](/wiki/CNAME_record "CNAME record") that points to a cluster of web servers. Since, currently[*[as of?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*], only a subdomain can be used in a CNAME, the same result cannot be achieved by using the bare domain root.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:World_Wide_Web#Dubious "Talk:World Wide Web")*]

When a user submits an incomplete domain name to a web browser in its address bar input field, some web browsers automatically try adding the prefix "www" to the beginning of it and possibly ".com", ".org" and ".net" at the end, depending on what might be missing. For example, entering "microsoft" may be transformed to *http://www.microsoft.com/* and "openoffice" to *http://www.openoffice.org*. This feature started appearing in early versions of [Firefox](/wiki/Firefox "Firefox"), when it still had the working title 'Firebird' in early 2003, from an earlier practice in browsers such as [Lynx](/wiki/Lynx_(web_browser) "Lynx (web browser)").[[58]](#cite_note-58)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] It is reported that Microsoft was granted a US patent for the same idea in 2008, but only for mobile devices.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Scheme specifiers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Scheme specifiers")]

The scheme specifiers *`http://`* and *`https://`* at the start of a web [URI](/wiki/Uniform_Resource_Identifier "Uniform Resource Identifier") refer to [Hypertext Transfer Protocol](/wiki/Hypertext_Transfer_Protocol "Hypertext Transfer Protocol") or [HTTP Secure](/wiki/HTTP_Secure "HTTP Secure"), respectively. They specify the communication protocol to use for the request and response. The HTTP protocol is fundamental to the operation of the World Wide Web, and the added encryption layer in HTTPS is essential when browsers send or retrieve confidential data, such as passwords or banking information. Web browsers usually automatically prepend http:// to user-entered URIs, if omitted.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Pages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Pages")]

Main article: [Web page](/wiki/Web_page "Web page")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Commons.png/250px-Commons.png)](/wiki/File:Commons.png)

A screenshot of the home page of Wikimedia Commons

A *web page* (also written as *webpage*) is a document that is suitable for the World Wide Web and [web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser"). A web browser displays a web page on a [monitor](/wiki/Computer_display "Computer display") or [mobile device](/wiki/Mobile_device "Mobile device").

The term *web page* usually refers to what is visible, but may also refer to the contents of the [computer file](/wiki/Computer_file "Computer file") itself, which is usually a [text file](/wiki/Text_file "Text file") containing [hypertext](/wiki/Hypertext "Hypertext") written in [HTML](/wiki/HTML "HTML") or a comparable [markup language](/wiki/Markup_language "Markup language"). Typical web pages provide [hypertext](/wiki/Hypertext "Hypertext") for browsing to other web pages via [hyperlinks](/wiki/Hyperlink "Hyperlink"), often referred to as *links*. Web browsers will frequently have to access multiple [web resource](/wiki/Web_resource "Web resource") elements, such as reading [style sheets](/wiki/Cascading_Style_Sheets "Cascading Style Sheets"), [scripts](/wiki/Client-side_scripting "Client-side scripting"), and images, while presenting each web page.

On a network, a web browser can retrieve a web page from a remote [web server](/wiki/Web_server "Web server"). The web server may restrict access to a private network, such as a corporate intranet. The web browser uses the [Hypertext Transfer Protocol](/wiki/Hypertext_Transfer_Protocol "Hypertext Transfer Protocol") (HTTP) to make such requests to the [web server](/wiki/Web_server "Web server").

A [*static* web page](/wiki/Static_web_page "Static web page") is delivered exactly as stored, as [web content](/wiki/Web_content "Web content") in the web server's [file system](/wiki/File_system "File system"). In contrast, a [*dynamic* web page](/wiki/Dynamic_web_page "Dynamic web page") is generated by a [web application](/wiki/Web_application "Web application"), usually driven by [server-side software](/wiki/Server-side_scripting "Server-side scripting"). Dynamic web pages are used when each user may require completely different information, for example, bank websites, web email, etc.

#### Static page

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Static page")]

Main article: [Static web page](/wiki/Static_web_page "Static web page")

A *static web page* (sometimes called a *flat page/stationary page*) is a [web page](/wiki/Web_page "Web page") that is delivered to the user exactly as stored, in contrast to [dynamic web pages](/wiki/Dynamic_web_page "Dynamic web page") which are generated by a [web application](/wiki/Web_application "Web application").

Consequently, a static web page displays the same information for all users, from all contexts, subject to modern capabilities of a [web server](/wiki/Web_server "Web server") to [negotiate](/wiki/Content_negotiation "Content negotiation") [content-type](/wiki/MIME_type "MIME type") or language of the document where such versions are available and the server is configured to do so.

#### Dynamic pages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Dynamic pages")]

Main articles: [Dynamic web page](/wiki/Dynamic_web_page "Dynamic web page") and [Ajax (programming)](/wiki/Ajax_(programming) "Ajax (programming)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Scheme_dynamic_page_en.svg/1280px-Scheme_dynamic_page_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Scheme_dynamic_page_en.svg)

Dynamic web page: example of server-side scripting ([PHP](/wiki/PHP "PHP") and [MySQL](/wiki/MySQL "MySQL"))

A *server-side dynamic web page* is a [web page](/wiki/Web_page "Web page") whose construction is controlled by an [application server](/wiki/Application_server "Application server") processing server-side scripts. In server-side scripting, [parameters](/wiki/Parameter_(computer_programming) "Parameter (computer programming)") determine how the assembly of every new web page proceeds, including the setting up of more client-side processing.

A *client-side dynamic web page* processes the web page using JavaScript running in the browser. JavaScript programs can interact with the document via [Document Object Model](/wiki/Document_Object_Model "Document Object Model"), or DOM, to query page state and alter it. The same client-side techniques can then dynamically update or change the DOM in the same way.

A dynamic web page is then reloaded by the user or by a [computer program](/wiki/Computer_program "Computer program") to change some variable content. The updated information could come from the server, or from changes made to that page's DOM. This may or may not truncate the browsing history or create a saved version to go back to, but a *dynamic web page update* using [Ajax](/wiki/Ajax_(programming) "Ajax (programming)") technologies will neither create a page to go back to nor truncate the [web browsing history](/wiki/Web_browsing_history "Web browsing history") forward of the displayed page. Using Ajax technologies, the end [user](/wiki/User_(computing) "User (computing)") gets *one dynamic page* managed as a single page in the [web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") while the actual [web content](/wiki/Web_content "Web content") rendered on that page can vary. The Ajax engine sits only on the browser requesting parts of its DOM, *the* DOM, for its client, from an application server.

Dynamic HTML, or DHTML, is the umbrella term for technologies and methods used to create web pages that are not [static web pages](/wiki/Static_web_page "Static web page"), though it has fallen out of common use since the popularisation of [AJAX](/wiki/Ajax_(programming) "Ajax (programming)"), a term which is now itself rarely used. Client-side scripting, server-side scripting, or a combination of these make for the dynamic web experience in a browser.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[JavaScript](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript") is a [scripting language](/wiki/Scripting_programming_language "Scripting programming language") that was initially developed in 1995 by [Brendan Eich](/wiki/Brendan_Eich "Brendan Eich"), then of [Netscape](/wiki/Netscape "Netscape"), for use within web pages.[[60]](#cite_note-Hamilton-60) The standardised version is [ECMAScript](/wiki/ECMAScript "ECMAScript").[[60]](#cite_note-Hamilton-60) To make web pages more interactive, some web applications also use JavaScript techniques such as [Ajax](/wiki/Ajax_(programming) "Ajax (programming)") ([asynchronous](/wiki/Asynchronous_I/O "Asynchronous I/O") JavaScript and [XML](/wiki/XML "XML")). [Client-side script](/wiki/Client-side_scripting "Client-side scripting") is delivered with the page that can make additional HTTP requests to the server, either in response to user actions such as mouse movements or clicks, or based on elapsed time. The server's responses are used to modify the current page rather than creating a new page with each response, so the server needs only to provide limited, incremental information. Multiple Ajax requests can be handled at the same time, and users can interact with the page while data is retrieved. Web pages may also regularly [poll](/wiki/Polling_(computer_science) "Polling (computer science)") the server to check whether new information is available.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### Website

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Website")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/United_States_Antarctic_Program_website_from_2018_02_22.png/250px-United_States_Antarctic_Program_website_from_2018_02_22.png)](/wiki/File:United_States_Antarctic_Program_website_from_2018_02_22.png)

The [usap.gov](/wiki/United_States_Antarctic_Program "United States Antarctic Program") website

Main article: [Website](/wiki/Website "Website")

A *website*[[62]](#cite_note-62) is a collection of related web resources including [web pages](/wiki/Web_page "Web page"), [multimedia](/wiki/Multimedia "Multimedia") content, typically identified with a common [domain name](/wiki/Domain_name "Domain name"), and published on at least one [web server](/wiki/Web_server "Web server"). Notable examples are [wikipedia](/wiki/Wikipedia "Wikipedia").org, [google](/wiki/Google "Google").com, and [amazon.com](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)").

A website may be accessible via a public [Internet Protocol](/wiki/Internet_Protocol "Internet Protocol") (IP) network, such as the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"), or a private [local area network](/wiki/Local_area_network "Local area network") (LAN), by referencing a [uniform resource locator](/wiki/URL "URL") (URL) that identifies the site.

Websites can have many functions and can be used in various fashions; a website can be a [personal website](/wiki/Personal_website "Personal website"), a corporate website for a company, a government website, an organisation website, etc. Websites are typically dedicated to a particular topic or purpose, ranging from entertainment and [social networking](/wiki/Social_networking "Social networking") to providing news and education. All publicly accessible websites collectively constitute the World Wide Web, while private websites, such as a company's website for its employees, are typically a part of an [intranet](/wiki/Intranet "Intranet").

Web pages, which are the building blocks of websites, are [documents](/wiki/Document "Document"), typically composed in [plain text](/wiki/Plain_text "Plain text") interspersed with [formatting instructions](/wiki/Formatted_text "Formatted text") of Hypertext Markup Language ([HTML](/wiki/HTML "HTML"), [XHTML](/wiki/XHTML "XHTML")). They may incorporate elements from other websites with suitable [markup anchors](/wiki/HTML_anchor "HTML anchor"). Web pages are accessed and transported with the [Hypertext Transfer Protocol](/wiki/Hypertext_Transfer_Protocol "Hypertext Transfer Protocol") (HTTP), which may optionally employ encryption ([HTTP Secure](/wiki/HTTP_Secure "HTTP Secure"), HTTPS) to provide security and privacy for the user. The user's application, often a [web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser"), renders the page content according to its HTML markup instructions onto a [display terminal](/wiki/Computer_monitor "Computer monitor").

[Hyperlinking](/wiki/Hyperlink "Hyperlink") between web pages conveys to the reader the [site structure](/wiki/Site_map "Site map") and guides the navigation of the site, which often starts with a [home page](/wiki/Home_page "Home page") containing a directory of the site [web content](/wiki/Web_content "Web content"). Some websites require user registration or [subscription](/wiki/Subscription "Subscription") to access content. Examples of [subscription websites](/wiki/Paywall "Paywall") include many business sites, news websites, [academic journal](/wiki/Academic_journal "Academic journal") websites, gaming websites, file-sharing websites, [message boards](/wiki/Internet_forum "Internet forum"), web-based [email](/wiki/Email "Email"), [social networking](/wiki/Social_networking "Social networking") websites, websites providing real-time price quotations for different types of markets, as well as sites providing various other services. [End users](/wiki/End_user "End user") can access websites on a range of devices, including [desktop](/wiki/Desktop_computer "Desktop computer") and [laptop computers](/wiki/Laptop "Laptop"), [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), and [smart TVs](/wiki/Smart_TV "Smart TV").

### Browser

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Browser")]

Main article: [Web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser")

A *web browser* (commonly referred to as a *browser*) is a [software](/wiki/Software_application "Software application") [user agent](/wiki/User_agent "User agent") for accessing information on the World Wide Web. To connect to a website's [server](/wiki/Web_server "Web server") and display its pages, a user needs to have a web browser program. This is the program that the user runs to download, format, and display a web page on the user's computer.

In addition to allowing users to find, display, and move between web pages, a web browser will usually have features like keeping bookmarks, recording history, managing cookies (see below), and home pages and may have facilities for recording passwords for logging into websites.

The most popular browsers are [Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome "Google Chrome"), [Safari](/wiki/Safari_(web_browser) "Safari (web browser)"), [Edge](/wiki/Microsoft_Edge "Microsoft Edge"), [Samsung Internet](/wiki/Samsung_Internet "Samsung Internet") and [Firefox](/wiki/Firefox "Firefox").[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### Server

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Server")]

Main article: [Web server](/wiki/Web_server "Web server")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Inside_and_Rear_of_Webserver.jpg/250px-Inside_and_Rear_of_Webserver.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inside_and_Rear_of_Webserver.jpg)

The inside and front of a [Dell PowerEdge](/wiki/Dell_PowerEdge "Dell PowerEdge") web server, a computer designed for [rack mounting](/wiki/Rack_mount "Rack mount")

A *Web server* is [server software](/wiki/Server_software "Server software"), or hardware dedicated to running said software, that can satisfy World Wide Web client requests. A web server can, in general, contain one or more websites. A web server processes incoming network requests over [HTTP](/wiki/Hypertext_Transfer_Protocol "Hypertext Transfer Protocol") and several other related protocols.

The primary function of a web server is to store, process and deliver [web pages](/wiki/Web_page "Web page") to [clients](/wiki/Client_(computing) "Client (computing)").[[64]](#cite_note-64) The communication between client and server takes place using the [Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP)](/wiki/Hypertext_Transfer_Protocol "Hypertext Transfer Protocol"). Pages delivered are most frequently [HTML documents](/wiki/HTML "HTML"), which may include [images](/wiki/Image "Image"), [style sheets](/wiki/Style_sheet_(web_development) "Style sheet (web development)") and [scripts](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript") in addition to the text content.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Wikimedia_Foundation_Servers-8055_35.jpg/250px-Wikimedia_Foundation_Servers-8055_35.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wikimedia_Foundation_Servers-8055_35.jpg)

Multiple web servers may be used for a high traffic website; here, [Dell](/wiki/Dell "Dell") servers are installed together to be used for the [Wikimedia Foundation](/wiki/Wikimedia_Foundation "Wikimedia Foundation").

A [user agent](/wiki/User_agent "User agent"), commonly a [web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") or [web crawler](/wiki/Web_crawler "Web crawler"), initiates communication by making a [request](/wiki/Hypertext_Transfer_Protocol#Request_message "Hypertext Transfer Protocol") for a specific resource using HTTP and the server responds with the content of that resource or an [error message](/wiki/List_of_HTTP_status_codes#4xx_client_errors "List of HTTP status codes") if unable to do so. The resource is typically a real file on the server's [secondary storage](/wiki/Secondary_memory "Secondary memory"), but this is not necessarily the case and depends on how the web server is [implemented](/wiki/Implementation "Implementation").

While the primary function is to serve content, full implementation of HTTP also includes ways of receiving content from clients. This feature is used for submitting [web forms](/wiki/Form_(web) "Form (web)"), including [uploading](/wiki/Upload "Upload") of files.

Many generic web servers also support [scripting](/wiki/Server-side_scripting "Server-side scripting") using [Active Server Pages](/wiki/Active_Server_Pages "Active Server Pages") (ASP), [PHP](/wiki/PHP "PHP") (Hypertext Preprocessor), or other [scripting languages](/wiki/Scripting_language "Scripting language"). This means that the behaviour of the web server can be scripted in separate files, while the actual server software remains unchanged. Usually, this function is used to generate HTML documents [dynamically](/wiki/Dynamic_web_page "Dynamic web page") ("on-the-fly") as opposed to returning [static documents](/wiki/Static_web_page "Static web page"). The former is primarily used for retrieving or modifying information from [databases](/wiki/Database "Database"). The latter is typically much faster and more easily [cached](/wiki/Web_cache "Web cache") but cannot deliver [dynamic content](/wiki/Dynamic_content "Dynamic content").

Web servers can also frequently be found [embedded](/wiki/Embedded_system "Embedded system") in devices such as [printers](/wiki/Printer_(computing) "Printer (computing)"), [routers](/wiki/Router_(computing) "Router (computing)"), [webcams](/wiki/Webcam "Webcam") and serving only a [local network](/wiki/Local_area_network "Local area network"). The web server may then be used as a part of a system for monitoring or administering the device in question. This usually means that no additional software has to be installed on the client computer since only a web browser is required (which now is included with most [operating systems](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system")).

### Optical networking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Optical networking")]

[Optical networking](/wiki/Optical_networking "Optical networking") is a sophisticated infrastructure that utilises optical fibre to transmit data over long distances, connecting countries, cities, and even private residences. The technology uses optical microsystems like [tunable lasers](/wiki/Tunable_laser "Tunable laser"), filters, [attenuators](/wiki/Attenuator_(electronics) "Attenuator (electronics)"), switches, and wavelength-selective switches to manage and operate these networks.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

The large quantity of optical fibre installed throughout the world at the end of the twentieth century set the foundation of the Internet as it is used today. The information highway relies heavily on optical networking, a method of sending messages encoded in light to relay information in various telecommunication networks.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

The [Advanced Research Projects Agency Network](/wiki/ARPANET "ARPANET") (ARPANET) was one of the first iterations of the Internet, created in collaboration with universities and researchers in 1969.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-www.merit.edu-71) However, access to the ARPANET was limited to researchers, and in 1985, the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation") founded the [National Science Foundation Network](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation_Network "National Science Foundation Network") (NSFNET), a program that provided supercomputer access to researchers.[[71]](#cite_note-www.merit.edu-71)

Limited public access to the Internet led to pressure from consumers and corporations to privatise the network. In 1993, the US passed the [National Information Infrastructure Act](/wiki/National_Information_Infrastructure "National Information Infrastructure"), which dictated that the National Science Foundation must hand over control of the optical capabilities to commercial operators.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)

The privatisation of the Internet and the release of the World Wide Web to the public in 1993 led to an increased demand for Internet capabilities. This spurred developers to seek solutions to reduce the time and cost of laying new fibre and increase the amount of information that can be sent on a single fibre, to meet the growing needs of the public.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

In 1994, Pirelli S.p.A.'s optical components division introduced a wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) system to meet growing demand for increased data transmission. This four-channel WDM technology allowed more information to be sent simultaneously over a single optical fibre, effectively boosting network capacity.[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79)

Pirelli wasn't the only company that developed a WDM system; another company, the [Ciena Corporation](/wiki/Ciena "Ciena") (Ciena), created its own technology to transmit data more efficiently. [David Huber](/wiki/David_R._Huber "David R. Huber"), an optical networking engineer and entrepreneur [Kevin Kimberlin](/wiki/Kevin_Kimberlin "Kevin Kimberlin") founded Ciena in 1992.[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) Drawing on laser technology from [Gordon Gould](/wiki/Gordon_Gould "Gordon Gould") and William Culver of [Optelecom, Inc.](/wiki/Optelecom "Optelecom"), the company focused on utilising optical amplifiers to transmit data via light.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) Under chief executive officer Pat Nettles, Ciena developed a dual-stage optical amplifier for dense wavelength-division multiplexing (DWDM), patented in 1997 and deployed on the Sprint network in 1996.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)

### Cookie

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Cookie")]

Main article: [HTTP cookie](/wiki/HTTP_cookie "HTTP cookie")

An *HTTP cookie* (also called *web cookie*, *Internet cookie*, *browser cookie*, or simply *cookie*) is a small piece of data sent from a website and stored on the user's computer by the user's [web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") while the user is browsing. Cookies were designed to be a reliable mechanism for websites to remember [stateful](/wiki/Program_state "Program state") information (such as items added in the shopping cart in an online store) or to record the user's browsing activity (including clicking particular buttons, [logging in](/wiki/Access_control "Access control"), or recording which pages were visited in the past). They can also be used to remember arbitrary pieces of information that the user previously entered into form fields, such as names, addresses, passwords, and credit card numbers.

Cookies perform essential functions in the modern web. Perhaps most importantly, *authentication cookies* are the most common method used by web servers to know whether the user is logged in or not, and which account they are logged in with. Without such a mechanism, the site would not know whether to send a page containing sensitive information or require the user to authenticate themselves by logging in. The security of an authentication cookie generally depends on the security of the issuing website and the user's [web browser](/wiki/Comparison_of_web_browsers#Vulnerabilities "Comparison of web browsers"), and on whether the cookie data is encrypted. Security vulnerabilities may allow a cookie's data to be read by a [hacker](/wiki/Hacker_(computer_security) "Hacker (computer security)"), used to gain access to user data, or used to gain access (with the user's credentials) to the website to which the cookie belongs (see [cross-site scripting](/wiki/Cross-site_scripting "Cross-site scripting") and [cross-site request forgery](/wiki/Cross-site_request_forgery "Cross-site request forgery") for examples).[[91]](#cite_note-91)

Tracking cookies, and especially third-party tracking cookies, are commonly used as ways to compile long-term records of individuals' browsing histories – a potential [privacy concern](/wiki/Internet_privacy#HTTP_cookies "Internet privacy") that prompted European[[92]](#cite_note-92) and U.S. lawmakers to take action in 2011.[[93]](#cite_note-eulaw-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) European law requires that all websites targeting [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") member states gain "informed consent" from users before storing non-essential cookies on their device.

Google [Project Zero](/wiki/Project_Zero_(Google) "Project Zero (Google)") researcher Jann Horn describes ways cookies can be read by [intermediaries](/wiki/Man-in-the-middle_attack "Man-in-the-middle attack"), like [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") hotspot providers. When in such circumstances, he recommends using the browser in [private browsing](/wiki/Private_browsing "Private browsing") mode (widely known as [Incognito mode](/wiki/Incognito_mode "Incognito mode") in Google Chrome).[[95]](#cite_note-95)

### Search engine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Search engine")]

Main article: [Search engine](/wiki/Search_engine "Search engine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Mayflower_Wikimedia_Commons_image_search_engine_screenshot.png/500px-Mayflower_Wikimedia_Commons_image_search_engine_screenshot.png)](/wiki/File:Mayflower_Wikimedia_Commons_image_search_engine_screenshot.png)

The results of a search for the term "lunar eclipse" in a web-based [image search](/wiki/Image_search "Image search") engine

A *web search engine* or *Internet search engine* is a [software system](/wiki/Software_system "Software system") that is designed to carry out *web search* (*Internet search*), which means to search the World Wide Web in a systematic way for particular information specified in a [web search query](/wiki/Web_search_query "Web search query"). The search results are generally presented in a line of results, often referred to as [search engine results pages](/wiki/Search_engine_results_page "Search engine results page") (SERPs). The information may be a mix of [web pages](/wiki/Web_page "Web page"), images, videos, infographics, articles, research papers, and other types of files. Some search engines also [mine data](/wiki/Data_mining "Data mining") available in [databases](/wiki/Database "Database") or [open directories](/wiki/Web_directory "Web directory"). Unlike [web directories](/wiki/Web_directories "Web directories"), which are maintained only by human editors, search engines also maintain [real-time](/wiki/Real-time_computing "Real-time computing") information by running an [algorithm](/wiki/Algorithm "Algorithm") on a [web crawler](/wiki/Web_crawler "Web crawler"). Internet content that is not capable of being searched by a web search engine is generally described as the [deep web](/wiki/Deep_web "Deep web").

In 1990, [Archie](/wiki/Archie_(search_engine) "Archie (search engine)"), the world's first search engine, was released. The technology was originally an index of [File Transfer Protocol](/wiki/File_Transfer_Protocol "File Transfer Protocol") (FTP) sites, which was a method for moving files between a client and a server network.[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97) This early search tool was superseded by more advanced engines like [Yahoo!](/wiki/Yahoo! "Yahoo!") in 1995 and [Google](/wiki/Google_Search "Google Search") in 1998.[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)

### Deep web

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Deep web")]

Main article: [Deep web](/wiki/Deep_web "Deep web")

The deep web,[[100]](#cite_note-nhamilton-100) *invisible web*,[[101]](#cite_note-jal-101) or *hidden web*[[102]](#cite_note-cthw-102) are parts of the World Wide Web whose contents are not [indexed](/wiki/Search_engine_indexing "Search engine indexing") by standard [web search engines](/wiki/Web_search_engine "Web search engine"). The opposite term to the deep web is the [surface web](/wiki/Surface_web "Surface web"), which is accessible to anyone using the Internet.[[103]](#cite_note-103) [Computer scientist](/wiki/Computer_scientist "Computer scientist") Michael K. Bergman is credited with coining the term *deep web* in 2001 as a search indexing term.[[104]](#cite_note-wright2009-104)

The content of the deep web is hidden behind [HTTP](/wiki/HTTP "HTTP") forms,[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106) and includes many very common uses such as [web mail](/wiki/Web_mail "Web mail"), [online banking](/wiki/Online_banking "Online banking"), and services that users must pay for, and which is protected by a [paywall](/wiki/Paywall "Paywall"), such as [video on demand](/wiki/Video_on_demand "Video on demand"), some online magazines and newspapers, among others.

The content of the deep web can be located and accessed by a direct [URL](/wiki/URL "URL") or [IP address](/wiki/IP_address "IP address") and may require a password or other security access past the public website page.

### Caching

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Caching")]

A [web cache](/wiki/Web_cache "Web cache") is a server computer located either on the public Internet or within an enterprise that stores recently accessed web pages to improve response time for users when the same content is requested within a certain time after the original request. Most web browsers also implement a [browser cache](/wiki/Browser_cache "Browser cache") by writing recently obtained data to a local data storage device. HTTP requests by a browser may ask only for data that has changed since the last access. Web pages and resources may contain expiration information to control caching to secure sensitive data, such as in [online banking](/wiki/Online_banking "Online banking"), or to facilitate frequently updated sites, such as news media. Even sites with highly dynamic content may permit basic resources to be refreshed only occasionally. Website designers find it worthwhile to collate resources such as CSS data and JavaScript into a few site-wide files so that they can be cached efficiently. Enterprise [firewalls](/wiki/Firewall_(networking) "Firewall (networking)") often cache Web resources requested by one user for the benefit of many users. Some [search engines](/wiki/Search_engines "Search engines") store cached content of frequently accessed websites.

## Security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Security")]

For [criminals](/wiki/Criminal "Criminal"), the Web has become a venue to spread [malware](/wiki/Malware "Malware") and engage in a range of [cybercrime](/wiki/Cybercrime "Cybercrime"), including (but not limited to) [identity theft](/wiki/Identity_theft "Identity theft"), [fraud](/wiki/Fraud "Fraud"), [espionage](/wiki/Espionage "Espionage"), and [intelligence gathering](/wiki/Intelligence_gathering "Intelligence gathering").[[107]](#cite_note-Ben-Itzhak-107) Web-based [vulnerabilities](/wiki/Vulnerability_(computing) "Vulnerability (computing)") now outnumber traditional computer security concerns,[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109) and as measured by [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), about one in ten web pages may contain malicious code.[[110]](#cite_note-110) Most web-based [attacks](/wiki/Attack_(computing) "Attack (computing)") take place on legitimate websites, and most, as measured by [Sophos](/wiki/Sophos "Sophos"), are hosted in the United States, China and Russia.[[111]](#cite_note-Sophos-Q1-2008-111) The most common of all malware [threats](/wiki/Threat_(computer) "Threat (computer)") is [SQL injection](/wiki/SQL_injection "SQL injection") attacks against websites.[[112]](#cite_note-112) Through HTML and URIs, the Web was vulnerable to attacks like [cross-site scripting](/wiki/Cross-site_scripting "Cross-site scripting") (XSS) that came with the introduction of JavaScript[[113]](#cite_note-FGHR-113) and were exacerbated to some degree by [Web 2.0](/wiki/Web_2.0 "Web 2.0") and Ajax [web design](/wiki/Web_design "Web design") that favours the use of scripts.[[114]](#cite_note-114) In one 2007 estimate, 70% of all websites are open to XSS attacks on their users.[[115]](#cite_note-115) [Phishing](/wiki/Phishing "Phishing") is another common threat to the Web. In February 2013, RSA (the security division of EMC) estimated the global losses from phishing at $1.5 billion in 2012.[[116]](#cite_note-First_Post-116) Two of the well-known phishing methods are Covert Redirect and Open Redirect.

Proposed solutions vary. Large security companies like [McAfee](/wiki/McAfee "McAfee") already design governance and compliance suites to meet post-9/11 regulations,[[117]](#cite_note-117) and some, like [Finjan Holdings](/wiki/Finjan_Holdings "Finjan Holdings") have recommended active real-time inspection of programming code and all content regardless of its source.[[107]](#cite_note-Ben-Itzhak-107) Some have argued that for enterprises to see Web security as a business opportunity rather than a [cost centre](/wiki/Cost_centre_(business) "Cost centre (business)"),[[118]](#cite_note-118) while others call for "ubiquitous, always-on [digital rights management](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management")" enforced in the infrastructure to replace the hundreds of companies that secure data and networks.[[119]](#cite_note-119) [Jonathan Zittrain](/wiki/Jonathan_Zittrain "Jonathan Zittrain") has said users sharing responsibility for computing safety is far preferable to locking down the Internet.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

## Privacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Privacy")]

Main article: [Internet privacy](/wiki/Internet_privacy "Internet privacy")

Every time a client requests a web page, the server can identify the request's [IP address](/wiki/IP_address "IP address"). Web servers usually log IP addresses in a [log file](/wiki/Log_file "Log file"). Also, unless set not to do so, most web browsers record requested web pages in a viewable *history* feature, and usually [cache](/wiki/Web_cache "Web cache") much of the content locally. Unless the server-browser communication uses HTTPS encryption, web requests and responses travel in plain text across the Internet and can be viewed, recorded, and cached by intermediate systems. Another way to hide [personally identifiable information](/wiki/Personally_identifiable_information "Personally identifiable information") is by using a [virtual private network](/wiki/Virtual_private_network "Virtual private network"). A VPN [encrypts](/wiki/Encryption "Encryption") traffic between the client and VPN server, and masks the original IP address, lowering the chance of user identification.

When a web page asks for, and the user supplies, personally identifiable information—such as their real name, address, e-mail address, etc. web-based entities can associate current web traffic with that individual. If the website uses [HTTP cookies](/wiki/HTTP_cookie "HTTP cookie"), username, and password authentication, or other tracking techniques, it can relate other web visits, before and after, to the identifiable information provided. In this way, a web-based organisation can develop and build a profile of the individual people who use its site or sites. It may be able to build a record for an individual that includes information about their leisure activities, their shopping interests, their profession, and other aspects of their [demographic profile](/wiki/Demographic_profile "Demographic profile"). These profiles are of potential interest to marketers, advertisers, and others. Depending on the website's [terms and conditions](/wiki/Terms_and_conditions "Terms and conditions") and the local laws that apply, information from these profiles may be sold, shared, or passed to other organisations without the user being informed. For many ordinary people, this means little more than some unexpected emails in their inbox or some uncannily relevant advertising on a future web page. For others, it can mean that time spent indulging an unusual interest can result in a deluge of further targeted marketing that may be unwelcome. Law enforcement, counterterrorism, and espionage agencies can also identify, target, and track individuals based on their interests or proclivities on the Web.

[Social networking](/wiki/Social_networking "Social networking") sites usually try to get users to use their real names, interests, and locations, rather than pseudonyms, as their executives believe that this makes the social networking experience more engaging for users. On the other hand, uploaded photographs or unguarded statements can be identified with an individual, who may regret this exposure. Employers, schools, parents, and other relatives may be influenced by aspects of social networking profiles, such as text posts or digital photos, that the posting individual did not intend for these audiences. [Online bullies](/wiki/Cyberbullying "Cyberbullying") may make use of personal information to harass or [stalk](/wiki/Cyberstalking "Cyberstalking") users. Modern social networking websites allow fine-grained control of the privacy settings for each posting, but these can be complex and not easy to find or use, especially for beginners.[[121]](#cite_note-121) Photographs and videos posted onto websites have caused particular problems, as they can add a person's face to an online profile. With modern and potential [facial recognition technology](/wiki/Facial_recognition_system "Facial recognition system"), it may then be possible to relate that face with other, previously anonymous, images, events, and scenarios that have been imaged elsewhere. Due to image caching, mirroring, and copying, it is difficult to remove an image from the World Wide Web.

## Standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Standards")]

Main article: [Web standards](/wiki/Web_standards "Web standards")

Web standards include many interdependent standards and specifications, some of which govern aspects of the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"), not just the World Wide Web. Even when not web-focused, such standards directly or indirectly affect the development and administration of websites and [web services](/wiki/Web_service "Web service"). Considerations include the [interoperability](/wiki/Interoperability "Interoperability"), [accessibility](/wiki/Accessibility "Accessibility") and [usability](/wiki/Usability "Usability") of web pages and websites.

Web standards, in the broader sense, consist of the following:

* *Recommendations* published by the [World Wide Web Consortium](/wiki/World_Wide_Web_Consortium "World Wide Web Consortium") (W3C)[[122]](#cite_note-122)
* "Living Standard" made by the [Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group](/wiki/Web_Hypertext_Application_Technology_Working_Group "Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group") (WHATWG)
* *[Request for Comments](/wiki/Request_for_Comments "Request for Comments")* (RFC) documents published by the [Internet Engineering Task Force](/wiki/Internet_Engineering_Task_Force "Internet Engineering Task Force") (IETF)[[123]](#cite_note-123)
* *Standards* published by the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") (ISO)[[124]](#cite_note-124)
* *Standards* published by [Ecma International](/wiki/Ecma_International "Ecma International") (formerly ECMA)[[125]](#cite_note-125)
* *The [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") Standard* and various *Unicode Technical Reports* (UTRs) published by the [Unicode Consortium](/wiki/Unicode_Consortium "Unicode Consortium")[[126]](#cite_note-126)
* Name and number registries maintained by the [Internet Assigned Numbers Authority](/wiki/Internet_Assigned_Numbers_Authority "Internet Assigned Numbers Authority") (IANA)[[127]](#cite_note-127)

Web standards are not fixed sets of rules but are constantly evolving sets of finalised technical specifications of web technologies.[[128]](#cite_note-128) Web standards are developed by [standards organisations](/wiki/Standards_organization "Standards organization")—groups of interested and often competing parties chartered with the task of standardisation—not technologies developed and declared to be a standard by a single individual or company. It is crucial to distinguish those specifications that are under development from the ones that already reached the final development status (in the case of [W3C](/wiki/W3C "W3C") specifications, the highest maturity level).

## Accessibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Accessibility")]

Main article: [Web accessibility](/wiki/Web_accessibility "Web accessibility")

There are methods for accessing the Web in alternative mediums and formats to facilitate use by individuals with [disabilities](/wiki/Disability "Disability"). These disabilities may be visual, auditory, physical, speech-related, cognitive, neurological, or some combination. Accessibility features also help people with temporary disabilities, like a broken arm, or ageing users as their abilities change.[[129]](#cite_note-WAI-129) The Web is receiving information as well as providing information and interacting with society. The World Wide Web Consortium claims that it is essential that the Web be accessible, so it can provide equal access and [equal opportunity](/wiki/Equal_opportunity "Equal opportunity") to people with disabilities.[[130]](#cite_note-130) Tim Berners-Lee once noted, "The power of the Web is in its universality. Access by everyone regardless of disability is an essential aspect."[[129]](#cite_note-WAI-129) Many countries regulate web accessibility as a requirement for websites.[[131]](#cite_note-131) International co-operation in the W3C [Web Accessibility Initiative](/wiki/Web_Accessibility_Initiative "Web Accessibility Initiative") led to simple guidelines that web content authors as well as software developers can use to make the Web accessible to persons who may or may not be using [assistive technology](/wiki/Assistive_technology "Assistive technology").[[129]](#cite_note-WAI-129)[[132]](#cite_note-132)

## Internationalisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Wide_Web&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Internationalisation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/World_Map_of_Web_Index_2014.svg/330px-World_Map_of_Web_Index_2014.svg.png)](/wiki/File:World_Map_of_Web_Index_2014.svg)

A global map of the [Web Index](/wiki/Web_Index "Web Index") for countries in 2014

The W3C [Internationalisation](/wiki/Internationalization_and_localization "Internationalization and localization") Activity assures that web technology works in all languages, scripts, and cultures.[[133]](#cite_note-133) Beginning in 2004 or 2005, [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") gained ground and eventually in December 2007 surpassed both [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII "ASCII") and Western European as the Web's most frequently used [character map](/wiki/Character_encoding#Code_pages "Character encoding").[[134]](#cite_note-134) Originally [RFC](/wiki/RFC_(identifier) "RFC (identifier)") [3986](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986) allowed resources to be identified by [URI](/wiki/URI "URI") in a subset of US-ASCII. [RFC](/wiki/RFC_(identifier) "RFC (identifier)") [3987](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3987) allows more characters—any character in the [Universal Character Set](/wiki/Universal_Character_Set "Universal Character Set")—and now a resource can be identified by [IRI](/wiki/Internationalized_Resource_Identifier "Internationalized Resource Identifier") in any language.[[135]](#cite_note-135)
